text: Secondly, several categories are reserved exclusively for particular backchannels. Backchannels are brief utterances that interrupt longer turns produced by another conversational participant (Yngve, 1970; Goodwin, 1986; Bavelas et al., 2000). In particular, the model assigns the backchannels “mhm,” “mm,” “nice,” and “awesome” to four separate categories. Prior research indicates that it is indeed beneficial to examine the effects of different backchannel types separately. For instance, Bavelas et al. text: Motivation: Pre-trained language models have achieved state-of-the-art results across a range of natural language processing tasks, including text classification, named entity recognition, and question answering. A major difficulty for practitioners is determining how to deploy these very large models in real-world settings. For example, models such as BERT Large (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), Megatron (Shoeybi et al., 2019), and T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) contain 340M, 1.5B, 8.3B, and 11B parameters, respectively. Although these models are trained offline, prediction still requires traversing the deep neural network stack with its vast number of parameters. This greatly increases both latency and memory demands.
source_text: Motivation: Pre-trained language models have shown state-of-the-art performance for various natural language processing applications like text classification, named entity recognition and questionanswering. A significant challenge facing practitioners is how to deploy these huge models in practice. For instance, models like BERT Large (Devlin et al., 2019) , GPT 2 (Radford et al., 2019) , Megatron (Shoeybi et al., 2019) and T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) have 340M , 1.5B, 8.3B and 11B parameters respectively. Although these models are trained offline, during prediction we need to traverse the deep neural network architecture stack involving a large number of parameters. This significantly increases latency and memory requirements.

text: Modern ASR systems rely on end-to-end, alignment free neural architectures, i.e. CTC (Graves et al., 2006) or sequence to sequence models (Graves et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2017) . The use of attention mechanisms significantly improve results in (Chorowski et al., 2015) and (Chan et al., 2016) . Recently, the success of transformer architectures for NLP tasks (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2019) has motivated speech researchers to investigate their efficacy in end-to-end ASR (Karita et al., 2019b) . Zhou et. al., apply an end-to-end transformer architecture for Mandarin Chinese ASR (Zhou et al., 2018) . Speech-Transformer extends the scaled dot-product attention mechanism to 2D and achieves competitive results for character level recognition Karita et al., 2019a) . Pham et. al. introduce the idea of stochastically deactivating layers dur-ing training to achieve a very deep model (Pham et al., 2019) . A major challenge of the transformer architecture is the quadratic memory complexity as a function of the input sequence length. Most architectures employ consecutive feature stacking (Pham et al., 2019) or CNN preprocessing Karita et al., 2019b) to downsample input feature vectors. Mohamed et al. (2019) use a VGG-based input network to downsample the input sequence and achieve learnable positional embeddings.

Baseline model Our baseline approach is a feature-based model of the type which has been used in previous research (Vajjala and Rama, 2018; Yannakoudakis et al., 2018) . text: We first encode screenplay scenes via a BiLSTM equipped with an attention mechanism. We then contextualize them with respect to the whole screenplay via a second BiLSTM. Next, we compute topic-aware scene representations t i via a context interaction layer (CIL) as proposed in Papalampidi et al. (2019) . CIL is inspired by traditional segmentation approaches (Hearst, 1997) and measures the semantic similarity of the current scene with a preceding and following context window in the screenplay. Hence, the topic-aware scene representations also encode the degree to which each scene acts as a topic boundary in the screenplay. In the final layer, we employ TP-specific attention mechanisms to compute the probability p i j that scene t i represents the j th TP in the screenplay. Note that we expect the TP-specific attention distributions to be sparse, as there are only a few scenes which are relevant for a TP (recall that TPs are boundary scenes between sections). text:
It’s scarce within a mortal’s pow’r  
At times to keep from turning sour,  
To mark how all things are dispensed;  
How best of men are often pinched,  
While fools on tens of thousands sprawl,  
And know not how to spend them all;  
But, Davie, lad, do not distress,  
Though little gear is ours to bless;  
We’re fit to win our daily bread,  
So long as we are stout and fed:  
“Ask no more, nor fear no more,”  
Old age ne’er heeds a fig;  
The worst of it, the end of it  
Is only just to beg.
source_text:
It’s hardly in a body’s pow’r
To keep, at times, frae being sour,
To see how things are shar’d;
How best o’ chiels are whiles in want,
While coofs on countless thousands rant,
And ken na how to wair’t;
But, Davie, lad, ne’er fash your head,
Tho’ we hae little gear;
We’re fit to win our daily bread,
As lang’s we’re hale and fier:
“Mair spier na, nor fear na,”
Auld age ne’er mind a feg;
The last o’t, the warst o’t
Is only but to beg.

text: A related but distinct research area, readability, examines the reading difficulty of a text body. This area has explored readability effects across a wide range of contexts, including online versus paper media (Kurniawan and Zaphiris, 2001), color and contrast (Legge et al., 1990), and writing style (Bostian, 1983). The most widely known readability metric for English, Flesch-Kincaid (Kincaid et al., 1975), determines readability using counts of syllables and words. Other researchers have sought to refine this straightforward metric, demonstrating successful reading-level classification with unigram language models (Si and Callan, 2001) or SVM models constructed from these basic textual features (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008). Since earlier metrics appear to be adequate, recent work has concentrated on evaluating and comparing a variety of metrics across different domains (Sugawara et al., 2017; Redmiles et al., 2019). We draw on this readability literature to inform our feature selection, as readability seems to be intrinsically tied to reading time. Accordingly, we use the py-readability-metrics package to incorporate seven state-of-the-art metrics, which we add to our data for the modeling task (Section 4, Appendix B).
source_text: A separate but similar line of research, readability, measures the reading difficulty of a body of text. This research area has investigated effects of readability in a plethora of areas: online vs paper (Kurniawan and Zaphiris, 2001), color and contrast (Legge et al., 1990) , and writing style (Bostian, 1983) . The most famous readability metric for English, the Flesch-Kincaid (Kincaid et al., 1975) , uses the number of syllables and words to determine readability. Other scientists have attempted to improve upon this simple metric, showing success in reading level classification with unigram language models (Si and Callan, 2001) or SVM models built on top of these basic textual characteristics (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008) . As previous metrics seem to be sufficient, recent research has focused on evaluating and comparing the diverse metrics on different domains (Sugawara et al., 2017; Redmiles et al., 2019) . We use these readability works to influence our choice of features, as readability seems inherently interwoven with reading time. We employ the py-readability-metrics package to include 7 state-of-the-art metrics that we add to our data for the modeling task (Section 4, Appendix B).

text: To address the second question, we conduct two analyses: (1) we perform a manual exploratory examination of model misclassifications on the most difficult test instances and group them into several linguistically motivated error categories; and (2) we translate these categories into testable hypotheses, which we then automatically evaluate on the full test set through adversarial rewriting—namely, by removing the suspected source of error and examining the resulting change in model prediction (Wu et al., 2019). We find that two classes of ambiguous relations account for most of the remaining errors. The dataset also includes cues that models without entity masking can exploit, for example, to classify relations correctly even when access to the sentential context is limited.
source_text: To answer the second question, we carry out two analyses: (1) we conduct a manual explorative analysis of model misclassifications on the most challenging test instances and categorize them into several linguistically motivated error categories; (2) we formulate these categories into testable hypotheses, which we can automatically validate on the full test set by adversarial rewriting -removing the suspected cause of error and observing the change in model prediction (Wu et al., 2019) . We find that two groups of ambiguous relations are responsible for most of the remaining errors. The dataset also contains clues that are exploited by models without entity masking, e.g. to correctly classify relations even with limited access to the sentential context.

text: Model compression and knowledge distillation: Earlier work in the vision community on large architectures such as AlexNet and ResNet has tackled this problem in two main ways. Studies on model compression apply quantization (Gong et al., 2014), low-precision training, and network pruning, as well as combinations of these methods (Han et al., 2016), to shrink the memory footprint. In contrast, knowledge distillation methods rely on student–teacher models. These methods include treating soft logits as supervision targets (Ba and Caruana, 2014), raising the softmax temperature to align it with the teacher’s output (Hinton et al., 2015), and using teacher representations (Romero et al., 2015) (see Cheng et al., 2017 for a survey), which imposes architectural constraints on the student, such as the embedding dimension. To mitigate this limitation, more recent work has combined task-specific distillation with pre-training the student model at an arbitrary embedding dimension, while still depending on transformer architectures (Turc et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). Izsak et al. (2019) and Shi et al. (2019) extend these ideas to sequence tagging for Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging and Named Entity Recognition (NER) in English. The work most closely related to ours is Tsai et al. (2019), which extends the same line of research to multilingual NER.
source_text: Model compression and knowledge distillation: Prior works in the vision community dealing with huge architectures like AlexNet and ResNet have addressed this challenge in two ways. Works in model compression use quantization (Gong et al., 2014) , low-precision training and pruning the network, as well as their combination (Han et al., 2016) to reduce the memory footprint. On the other hand, works in knowledge distillation leverage student teacher models. These approaches include using soft logits as targets (Ba and Caruana, 2014) , increasing the temperature of the softmax to match that of the teacher (Hinton et al., 2015) as well as using teacher representations (Romero et al., 2015 ) (refer to (Cheng et al., 2017 ) for a survey). of the teacher models which adds architectural constraints (e.g., embedding dimension) on the student. In order to address this shortcoming, more recent works combine task-specific distillation with pre-training the student model with arbitrary embedding dimension but still relying on transformer architectures (Turc et al., 2019) ; (Jiao et al., 2019) ; (Zhao et al., 2019) . Izsak et al. (2019) ; Shi et al. (2019) extend these for sequence tagging for Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging and Named Entity Recognition (NER) in English. The one closest to our work Tsai et al. (2019) extends the above for multilingual NER.

text: To validate our approach, we trained the baseline models both with and without the auxiliary loss objectives described in §3.3 and §3.4. In this setup, we used the CoNLL 2003 (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003) and GermEval 2014 (Benikova et al., 2014) datasets. The baselines included GloVe vectors combined with FLAIR and character embeddings (FLAIR + GloVe, GloVe + Char), BERT, and ELMo embeddings for English. For German, we used Wikipedia FastText vectors together with FLAIR and character embeddings (FLAIR + Wiki, Wiki + Char). We adopted a label-preserving training configuration (α = 1.0, ηtrain = 10%). Table 1 reports the results of this experiment. We observed that our auxiliary training objectives improved accuracy on noisy input data for all baseline models in both languages. At the same time, they maintained accuracy on the original input. The data augmentation objective appeared to perform slightly better than the stability objective. However, the selected hyperparameter values were somewhat arbitrary, since our aim was to demonstrate the usefulness and flexibility of both objectives.
source_text: To validate our approach, we trained the baseline models with and without our auxiliary loss objectives ( §3.3, §3.4) 9 . We used the CoNLL 2003 (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003) and the GermEval 2014 (Benikova et al., 2014) data sets in this setup 10 . The baselines utilized GloVe vectors coupled with FLAIR and character embeddings (FLAIR + GloVe, GloVe + Char), BERT, and ELMo embeddings for English. For German, we employed Wikipedia FastText vectors paired with FLAIR and character embeddings (FLAIR + Wiki, Wiki + Char) 11 . We used a label-preserving training setup (α = 1.0, η train = 10%). Table 1 presents the results of this experiment 12 . We found that our auxiliary training objectives boosted accuracy on noisy input data for all baseline models and both languages. At the same time, they preserved accuracy for the original input. The data augmentation objective seemed to perform slightly better than the stability objective. However, the chosen hyper-parameter values were rather ar-bitrary, as our goal was to prove the utility and the flexibility of both objectives.

text: Identifying BABY-STEPs Our learning approach requires an agent to follow microinstructions (i.e., the BABY-STEPs). Existing datasets (Anderson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019) do not provide fine-grained segmentations of long instructions. Therefore, we use a template matching approach to aggregate consecutive sentences into BABY-STEPs. First, we extract the noun phrase using POS tagging. text: Entities of Interest : In the interest of brevity, let us define "Entities of interest" φ(x) as the set of all entity predictions that can be queried from p θ for a sample x. For instance, in the case of answer span prediction for QA, the φ(x) may contain the MAP prediction of the best answer span (answer start and end indexes). In a parsing or sequence labeling task, φ(x) may contain the top-k label sequences obtained from viterbi decoding. In a relation or named-entity extraction task, φ(x) contains the relation or named entity span predictions respectively. Each entity s in φ(x) corresponds to a event set E that is defined by all outputs in Y that contain the entity s. I(x) contains set E for all entities in φ(x).

The input features are 80-dimensional log Mel spectrograms, that is, \(x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{80}\). Both RNN and RNN aux consist of a 3-layer, 512-dimensional unidirectional GRU (Cho et al., 2014) with residual connections across two successive layers. Accordingly, \(W, W_{\mathrm{aux}} \in \mathbb{R}^{512 \times 80}\). We set \(\lambda = 0.1\) and the sampling probability \(P = 0.15\), meaning that each frame has a 15% chance of being chosen as an anchor position. The values of \(P\) and \(\lambda\) are determined according to the validation loss of \(L_f\) on a small data split. All models are trained for 100 epochs with Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015), using a batch size of 32 and a learning rate of \(10^{-3}\).

text: 2) Image based features: Since the word likelihood values received from language models are in essence signals, we explore signal detection approaches as well. For example, for audio classifi-cation (Hershey et al., 2017) store plots of the logmel spectogram of the audios as images and then apply image classification methods. VGG (Simonyan and Zisserman, 2014), was one of the top performers of the different classifiers they tested. Inspired by them, we explore obfuscation detection via image classification. Specifically, we explore a transfer learning approach wherein we use the VGG-19 classifier 4 trained for image classification on ImageNet dataset 5 . For our method, we sort the extracted likelihood values for the text in descending order and then plot these values saving it as an image. This image is then processed by the pre-trained VGG-19. We extract the document's 6 representation from the last flatten layer of VGG-19 (before the fully connected layers) as it contains high-level information regarding edges and patterns in the image. We expect this resulting feature representation vector to capture information regarding text smoothness.

text: For RL-based text generation, most existing works rely on a model-free framework, which has been criticized for its high variance and poor sample efficiency (Sutton and Barto, 1998) . On the other hand, while model-based RL methods do not suffer from these issues, they are usually difficult to train in complex environments. Further, a learned policy is usually restricted by the capacity of an environment model. Recent developments on model-based RL (Gu et al., 2016; Kurutach et al., 2018; Nagabandi et al., 2017) combine the advantages of these two approaches, and have achieved improved performance by learning a model-free policy, assisted by an environment model. In addition, model-based RL has been employed recently to solve problems with extremely sparse rewards, with curiosity-driven methods (Pathak et al., 2017) .

Another way in which SUGILITE exploits GUI groundings in natural language instructions is by inferring task parameters and their possible values. This enables the agent to learn generalized procedures—for example, how to order any type of beverage from Starbucks—from a demonstration of a specific task instance, such as ordering an iced cappuccino. SUGILITE accomplishes this by matching the user utterance (e.g., “order a cup of iced cappuccino”) with the data descriptions of the target UI elements (e.g., click on the menu item that has the text “Iced Cappuccino”) and with the arguments of the demonstrated actions (e.g., put “Iced Cappuccino” into a search box) to find correspondences. Through this process, different parts of the utterance are grounded to specific actions in the demonstrated procedure. It then examines the hierarchical GUI structure at the time of demonstration and searches for alternative GUI elements that are structurally parallel to the original target GUI element. In this manner, it derives other possible values for the identified parameter, such as the names of all the other drinks shown in the same menu as “Iced Cappuccino”.
source_text: Another way SUGILITE leverages GUI groundings in the natural language instructions is to infer task parameters and their possible values. This allows the agent to learn generalized procedures (e.g., to order any kind of beverage from Starbucks) from a demonstration of a specific instance of the task (e.g., ordering an iced cappuccino). SUGILITE achieves this by comparing the user utterance (e.g., "order a cup of iced cappuccino") against the data descriptions of the target UI elements (e.g., click on the menu item that has the text "Iced Cappuccino") and the arguments (e.g., put "Iced Cappuccino" into a search box) of the demonstrated actions for matches. This process grounds different parts in the utterances to specific actions in the demonstrated procedure. It then analyzes the hierarchical structure of GUI at the time of demonstration, and looks for alternative GUI elements that are in parallel to the original target GUI element structurally. In this way, it extracts the other possible values for the identified parameter, such as the names of all the other drinks displayed in the same menu as "Iced Cappuccino"

text: For many NLP applications, the most widely used transfer learning approaches begin by pre-training a large language model, such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018), GPT (Radford et al., 2019), and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Such a language model is able to encode broad syntactic and semantic information that can then be leveraged in downstream NLP tasks. It is especially appealing because it can be trained entirely in an unsupervised way using very large quantities of unlabeled data, which are now extremely inexpensive to obtain from the internet. The resulting massively large multidomain text corpus makes it possible to train very large language models. To the best of our knowledge, the largest language model to date is T5, with an enormous scale of roughly 11 billion parameters (Raffel et al., 2019).
source_text: For many applications in NLP, most popular transfer learning methods choose to pre-train a large language model, e.g., ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) , GPT (Radford et al., 2019) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) . Such a language model can capture general semantic and syntactic information that can be further used in downstream NLP tasks. The language model is particularly attractive, because it can be trained in a completely unsupervised manner with huge amount of unlabeled data, which are extremely cheap to fetch from internet nowadays. The resulting extremely large multidomain text corpus allows us to train huge language models. To the best of our knowledge, by far the largest language model, T5, has an enormous size of about 11 billion parameters (Raffel et al., 2019) .

Model-based or model-free text generation aims to produce the next word (action) conditioned on the current (sub-)sentence (state). The generator is treated as an agent that learns a policy for predicting the next word from its current state. In prior work (Ranzato et al., 2016), a metric reward is assigned and the generator is trained solely to maximize this reward through trial, and this therefore constitutes model-free learning. In the proposed approach, the guider network captures the environment dynamics and is trained by minimizing the cosine similarity between its prediction and the ground truth on real text. During generator training, the model maximizes a reward determined by both the metric and the guider network, and thus the method is model-free learning with model-based boosting (Gu et al., 2016). Our method also incorporates a model predictive control scheme, in which the guider network assists next-word selection at each time step.
source_text: Model-based or Model-free Text generation seeks to generate the next word (action) given the current (sub-)sentence (state). The generator is considered as an agent that learns a policy to predict the next word given its current state. In previous work (Ranzato et al., 2016) , a metric reward is given and the generator is trained to only maximize the metric reward by trial, thus this is model-free learning. In the proposed method, the guider network models the environment dynamics, and is trained by minimizing the cosine similarity between the prediction and the ground truth on real text. For generator training, it maximizes the reward which is determined by the metric and guider network, and thus is model-free learning with model-based boosting (Gu et al., 2016) . The model predictive control scheme is included in our method, where the guider network is used to help next-word selection at each time-step.

text: We note that semi-structured premises compelled annotators to invoke world knowledge and common sense (KCS); 48.75% of instances in the Dev set require KCS. By comparison, in the MultiNLI corpus, KCS is required for 25.72% of examples. We suspect that this is because information about the entities and their types is not explicitly provided in tables and must therefore be inferred. In making these inferences, our annotators drew on their knowledge of the world, including facts about weather, seasons, and widely recognized social and cultural norms and facts. One illustration of such common sense is the hypothesis that “X was born in summer” for a person whose date of birth is in May in New York. We expect that the INFOTABS data can serve as a foundation for investigating common-sense reasoning, alongside other recent work such as that of Talmor et al. (2019),Neutral hypotheses are more likely to be subjective or out-of-table because nearly anything subjective, or anything not mentioned in the table, is a neutral statement. Even so, we found that in all evaluations in Appendix E, except those involving the adversarial α 2 test set, our models found neutrals almost as difficult as the other two labels, with only an ≈ 3% gap between the F-scores of the neutral label and the next best label.

We expect that the INFOTABS data can serve as a basis for studying common sense reasoning alongside other recent work such as that of Talmor et al. text: Modern ASR systems are based on end-to-end, alignment-free neural architectures, namely CTC (Graves et al., 2006) and sequence-to-sequence models (Graves et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2017). The incorporation of attention mechanisms substantially improves performance in (Chorowski et al., 2015) and (Chan et al., 2016). More recently, the strong performance of transformer architectures on NLP tasks (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2019) has led speech researchers to examine their effectiveness for end-to-end ASR (Karita et al., 2019b). Zhou et al. apply an end-to-end transformer architecture to Mandarin Chinese ASR (Zhou et al., 2018). Speech-Transformer extends scaled dot-product attention to two dimensions and attains competitive results for character-level recognition (Karita et al., 2019a). Pham et al. introduce stochastic layer deactivation during training in order to build a very deep model (Pham et al., 2019). A key limitation of the transformer architecture is its quadratic memory complexity with respect to input sequence length. Most architectures therefore use consecutive feature stacking (Pham et al., 2019) or CNN preprocessing (Karita et al., 2019b) to downsample input feature vectors. Mohamed et al. (2019) employ a VGG-based input network to downsample the input sequence and obtain learnable positional embeddings.

text: Structured knowledge graphs encode rich semantics through entities and relations (Hayashi et al., 2019). Many earlier studies have concentrated on task-oriented dialogue systems built on domain-specific knowledge bases (Zhu et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2016). To produce responses from a large-scale knowledge base, Zhou et al. (2018a) and Liu et al. (2018) employ graph attention and knowledge diffusion to choose knowledge semantics for utterance understanding and response generation. Moon et al. (2019) addresses the entity selection task and leverages positive entities that occur in the gold response. In contrast to prior work, Concept-Flow explicitly models the conversation flow with a commonsense knowledge graph and proposes a novel attention mechanism over all concepts to steer the conversation flow in latent concept space.
source_text: The structured knowledge graphs include rich semantics represented via entities and relations (Hayashi et al., 2019) . Lots of previous studies focus on task-targeted dialog systems based on domain-specific knowledge bases Zhu et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2016) . To generate responses with a large-scale knowledge base, Zhou et al. (2018a) and Liu et al. (2018) utilize graph attention and knowledge diffusion to select knowledge semantics for utterance understanding and response generation. Moon et al. (2019) focuses on the task of entity selection, and takes advantage of positive entities that appear in the golden response. Different from previous research, Concept-Flow models the conversation flow explicitly with the commonsense knowledge graph and presents a novel attention mechanism on all concepts to guide the conversation flow in the latent concept space.

The expenses
Of gathering shipmoney, and of distraining
For every petty rate (for we encounter
A desperate opposition inch by inch
In every warehouse and on every farm),
Have swallowed up the gross sum of the imposts;
So that, though felt as a most grievous scourge
Upon the land, they stand us in small stead
As touches the receipt.

text: In order to calculate mean log lexical frequency on the DementiaBank narratives, we used the SUBTLEX us corpus shown to produce lexical frequencies more consistent with psycholinguistic measures of word processing time than those calculated from the Celex corpus (Brysbaert and New, 2009) . The DementiaBank narratives were processed using NLTK's 4 implementation of the TnT part-of-speech tagger (Brants, 2000) trained on the Brown corpus (Francis and Kucera, 1979) . Following Bird et al. (2000) only nouns and verbs unique within the narrative were used to calculate mean log lexical frequency. To validate the mean log lexical frequency values obtained with the SUBTLEX us corpus, we compared the log lexical frequency means for the six narratives developed by Bird et al. text: Baseline. Our baseline is the self-attention model for text infilling (Zhu et al., 2019). It is a transformer language model with a novel positional encoding scheme. The standard method of representing the absolute position of each token is not directly usable for our task, since the absolute positions of contextual tokens after the missing sentence are not known beforehand. To address this problem, Zhu et al. (2019) partition the text into segments. When there is only one masked sentence, the first (third) segment contains the contextual tokens before (after) the mask, and the second segment corresponds to the masked span. Each token is then indexed by its segment ID and its position ID within that segment. The missing tokens are generated sequentially from these IDs and the current surrounding context. When training the baseline model on our dataset, we follow the same hyperparameters as in the original paper, except that the batch size is reduced to 250 (it is 400 in Zhu et al., 2019), which prevents out-of-memory errors. It should be noted that we work with substantially longer sequences, usually exceeding 100 tokens, whereas Zhu et al. (2019) consider sequences with a maximum length of only 16 tokens.
source_text: Baseline. Our baseline is the self-attention model for text infilling (Zhu et al., 2019) . It is a transformer language model with novel positional encoding. The traditional approach of encoding the absolute position of each token is not directly applicable to our task because we do not know in advance the absolute positions of contextual tokens after the missing sentence. To resolve this issue, (Zhu et al., 2019) divides the text into segments. In the case of only one masked sentence, the first (third) segment consists of contextual tokens before (after) the mask, and the second corresponds to the mask. Then, each token is indexed by its segment ID and its position ID within the segment. The missing tokens are sequentially generated from these IDs and the current surrounding context. Training the baseline model on our dataset, we use the same set of hyperparameters as in the original reference except that the batch size is set to 250 (it is 400 in (Zhu et al., 2019) ). This avoids out-ofmemory errors. Note that we are handling much longer sequences (usually > 100 tokens) than (Zhu et al., 2019) , in which the maximum number of tokens in a sequence is only 16.

text: Perplexity is employed as an estimate of the degree of fit between a probabilistic language model and a segment of previously unseen text. The idea of using the perplexity of an n-gram model, which is a derivative of cross-entropy, as a proxy for syntactic complexity in spoken narratives was introduced by Roark et al. (2007) and later applied to transcribed responses from logical memory (story recall) tests produced by patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI: a common precursor to an AD diagnosis). In that study, sequences of part-of-speech (POS) tags were used to train bi-gram models on logical memory narratives, after which the cross-entropy of these models was evaluated on held-out cross-validation folds. The authors reported significantly higher mean cross-entropy values in the narratives of MCI patients than in those of controls. Later work extended the use of POS cross-entropy as one of the linguistic features in a predictive model for MCI detection (Roark et al., 2011).
source_text: Perplexity is used as an estimate of the fit between a probabilistic language model and a segment of pre-viously unseen text. The notion of applying n-gram model perplexity (a derivative of cross-entropy) as a surrogate measure of syntactic complexity in spoken narratives was proposed by Roark et al. (2007) and applied to transcribed logical memory (story recall) test responses by patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI: a frequent precursor to AD diagnosis). In this work, sequences of part-of-speech (POS) tags were used to train bi-gram models on logical memory narratives, and then cross-entropy of these models was computed on held-out crossvalidation folds. They found significantly higher mean cross-entropy values in narratives of MCI patients as compared to controls. Subsequent work expanded the use of POS cross-entropy as one of the language characteristics in a predictive model for detecting MCI (Roark et al., 2011) .

text: Reference-free MT evaluation. Recently, there has been a growing interest in reference-free MT evaluation (Ma et al., 2019) , also referred to as "quality estimation" (QE) in the MT community. In this setup, evaluation metrics semantically compare system translations directly to the source sentences. The attractiveness of automatic referencefree MT evaluation is obvious: it does not require any human effort or parallel data. To approach this task, Popović et al. (2011) exploit a bag-ofword translation model to estimate translation quality, which sums over the likelihoods of aligned word-pairs between source and translation texts. Specia et al. (2013) estimate translation quality using language-agnostic linguistic features extracted from source lanuage texts and system translations. Lo et al. (2014) introduce XMEANT as a crosslingual reference-free variant of MEANT, a metric based on semantic frames. Lo (2019) extended this idea by leveraging M-BERT embeddings. The resulting metric, YiSi-2, evaluates system translations by summing similarity scores over words pairs that are best-aligned mutual translations. YiSi-2-SRL optionally combines an additional similarity score based on the alignment over the semantic structures (e.g., semantic roles and frames). Both metrics are reference-free, but YiSi-2-SRL is not resource-lean as it requires a semantic parser for both languages. Moreover, in contrast to our proposed metrics, they do not mitigate the misalignment of cross-lingual embedding spaces and do not integrate a target-side language model, which we identify to be crucial components.

They were two cousins, near as twins to view,  
Save that from out the calendar of sins  
Nature had blotted their affection—torn  
Asunder only by their births forlorn.  
And so together they upgrew, like flowers  
That on one stem drink one sun, one rain of showers,  
That in their purple season either lull  
Or wake, the same hand gathering when full;  
The same fair clime shall wither them in death.  
This lovely day smiles upon all that breathe  
In love—and who loved ever as didst thou,  
Fiordispina? Hardly Cosimo, now  
Whose heart and brain are kindled through and through  
With such a vision as eclipses view  
Of that fair idol from whose form it came.  
He faints, dissolved in a great sea of flame;  
But thou art like a planet, throned above;  
But thou art very Love, that rules the move  
Of his enthralled soul: such passion must  
Find sin and sorrow, had not gentle August May  
Brought forth this morn—your wedding-day.
source_text:
They were two cousins, almost like to twins,
Except that from the catalogue of sins
Nature had rased their love—which could not be
But by dissevering their nativity.
And so they grew together like two flowers
Upon one stem, which the same beams and showers
Lull or awaken in their purple prime,
Which the same hand will gather—the same clime
Shake with decay. This fair day smiles to see
All those who love—and who e’er loved like thee,
Fiordispina? Scarcely Cosimo,
Within whose bosom and whose brain now glow
The ardours of a vision which obscure
The very idol of its portraiture.
He faints, dissolved into a sea of love;
But thou art as a planet sphered above;
But thou art Love itself—ruling the motion
Of his subjected spirit: such emotion
Must end in sin and sorrow, if sweet May
Had not brought forth this morn—your wedding-day.

text: Context and significance. Learning to assign labels to text sequences is among the most extensively studied problems in natural language processing. More recently, neural networks have been used not only for sequential labeling tasks such as part-of-speech tagging (Plank et al., 2016) and named entity recognition (Ma and Hovy, 2016), but also to reformulate inherently non-sequential tasks, such as syntactic parsing, within a labeling framework (Gómez-Rodríguez and Vilares, 2018; Strzyz et al., 2019).
source_text: Context and relevance. Learning to label sequences of text is one of the more thoroughly explored topics in natural language processing. In recent times, neural networks are applied not only to sequential labeling like part-of-speech tagging (Plank et al., 2016) or named entity recognition (Ma and Hovy, 2016) , but also to cast into a labeling framework otherwise non-sequential tasks such as syntactic parsing (Gómez-Rodríguez and Vilares, 2018; Strzyz et al., 2019) .

text:
How blessed the hermit’s portion,
Who, all forgetting, all forgot,
Within his lowly cell,
The cavern wild with interwoven roots,
Sits o’er his freshly gathered fruits,
Beside his crystal well!

Or, perhaps, to his evening musing,
By some untraveled stream,
The ways of men come drifting in,
A faint and gathered dream;
While praising, and raising
His thoughts to heaven on high,
As, wandering, meandering,
He looks upon the solemn sky.
source_text:
How blest the solitary’s lot,
Who, all-forgetting, all forgot,
Within his humble cell,
The cavern, wild with tangling roots,
Sits o’er his newly gather’d fruits,
Beside his crystal well!
Or haply, to his ev’ning thought,
By unfrequented stream,
The ways of men are distant brought,
A faint, collected dream;
While praising, and raising
His thoughts to heav’n on high,
As wand’ring, meand’ring,
He views the solemn sky.

text: Annotation-based Evaluation. Some approaches directly instruct human annotators to score summaries according to specific guidelines, such as Responsiveness, which assesses the overall quality of summaries in terms of relevance, fluency, and readability, and Pyramid (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004), which evaluates summary relevance. More recently, systems have been introduced to facilitate the construction of Pyramid scores, for example (Hirao et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2019b; , but they still depend on human-annotated Summary Content Units (SCUs) in order to generate reliable scores. In addition to SCUs, recent research has investigated eliciting preferences over summaries (Zopf, 2018; Gao et al., 2018 Gao et al., 2019a) and annotations of important bi-grams (P.V.S and Meyer, 2017) as a basis for deriving summary ratings.
source_text: Annotation-based Evaluation. Some methods directly ask human annotators to rate summaries following some guidelines, e.g. Responsiveness, which measures the overall quality (relevance, fluency and readability) of summaries, and Pyramid (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004) , which measures summaries' relevance. Recently, systems have been developed to ease the construction of Pyramid scores, e.g. (Hirao et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2019b; , but they still require human-annotated Summary Content Units (SCUs) to produce reliable scores. Besides SCUs, recent work has explored eliciting preferences over summaries (Zopf, 2018; Gao et al., 2018 Gao et al., , 2019a and annotations of important bi-grams (P.V.S and Meyer, 2017) to derive summary ratings.

text: “Can you wonder that such thoughts transported me with rage? I only wonder that at that moment, instead of venting my sensations in exclamations and agony, I did not rush among mankind and perish in the attempt to destroy them.

I looked, and lo! one rose before me, eloquent.  
His eyes were dark and deep, and the clear brow  
That overhung them seemed like the morning sky,  
The cloudless Heaven of Spring, when in their flow  
Through the bright air, the gentle winds that blow  
Stir the green world awake—his gestures still obeyed  
The speaking mind that lit his features so,  
And where his curved lips lay half ajar,  
Passion’s most divine tide had forced its ardent way.
source_text:
I looked, and lo! one stood forth eloquently.
His eyes were dark and deep, and the clear brow
Which shadowed them was like the morning sky,
The cloudless Heaven of Spring, when in their flow
Through the bright air, the soft winds as they blow
Wake the green world—his gestures did obey
The oracular mind that made his features glow,
And where his curved lips half-open lay,
Passion’s divinest stream had made impetuous way.

text: Following the setting of (Hu et al., 2017), we primarily consider short text generation, with outputs no longer than 15 tokens, since this task is more amenable to both automatic and human evaluation. We conduct experiments on Yelp (Shen et al., 2017) and News Titles (Fu et al., 2018). Yelp is a corpus of restaurant reviews. We adopt the preprocessed version used in (Shen et al., 2017), which includes two polarity sentiment labels. For News Titles, we select titles from the Business, Entertainment, and Health categories for our experiments. Both Yelp and News Titles consist of relatively short texts. We remove texts longer than 15 words and then retain the top 8,900 and 10,000 words as the vocabularies for Yelp and News Titles, respectively. The dataset statistics are reported in Table 1. We discard the labels in the original training and validation splits. The original training split is used as the unlabeled corpus, the validation split is used to choose the best unsupervised models, and the test split is treated as the labeled conditional text.
source_text: Following the setting of (Hu et al., 2017) , we mainly focus on short text generation (no longer than 15 tokens), which is easier for both automatic and human evaluations. For News Titles, we choose the titles belong to Business, Entertainment and Health categories for our experiments. Both Yelp and News Titles are datasets with relatively short text. We filter out text longer than 15 words, then choose the top 8,900 and 10,000 words as the vocabulary for Yelp and News Titles, respectively. The statistics of the two datasets are listed in Table 1 . We discard the labels in the original training and validation splits. text: We perform experiments on image captioning (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) to examine the gains introduced by the Guider network. In the image captioning task, rather than employing a discriminator to specify the final rewards for the generated sentence, we use evaluation metrics computed from human references. These final rewards are seemingly more influential, since they incorporate reference, i.e., ground-truth, information. The feature-matching rewards serve as a regularizer for the final rewards. In this setting, we refer to our model as a guider-matching sequence training (GMST) model. An overview of GMST is given in the Appendix. We evaluate the proposed model on the MS COCO dataset (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015), which contains 123,287 images in total. Each image is annotated with at least five captions. Following Karpathy's split (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015), 5,000 images are used for validation and another 5,000 for testing. We report BLEU-k scores (k ranging from 1 to 4), CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015), and ME-TEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005). We consider two configurations: (i) using a pre-trained 152-layer ResNet (He et al., 2016) for feature extraction, where we take the output of the 2048-dimensional pool5 layer from ResNet-152 pretrained on ImageNet; and (ii) using semantic tags detected from the image as features. We use an LSTM with 512 hidden units and mini-batches of size 64. For optimization, we employ Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) with a learning rate of 2 × 10−4. We first pretrain the captioning model for up to 20 epochs, then train it with reinforcement learning for 20 additional epochs, and finally test the best model on the validation set.
source_text: We conduct experiments on image captioning (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , investigating benefits brought by the Guider network. In image captioning, instead of using a discriminator to define final rewards for generated sentence, we adopt evaluation metrics computed based on human references. The final rewards appear more important as they contain reference (ground-truth) information. Feature-matching rewards work as a regularizer of the final rewards. We call our model in this setting a guider-matching sequence training (GMST) model. An overview of GMST is provided in the Appendix. We test our proposed model on the MS COCO dataset (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , containing 123,287 images in total. Each image is annotated with at least 5 captions. Following Karpathy's split (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , 5,000 images are used for both validation and testing. We report BLEU-k (k from 1 to 4), CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015) , and ME-TEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) scores. We consider two settings: (i) using a pre-trained 152layer ResNet (He et al., 2016) for feature extraction, where we take the output of the 2048-way pool5 layer from ResNet-152, pretrained on the ImageNet dataset; and (ii) using semantic tags detected from the image as features . We use an LSTM with 512 hidden units with mini-batches of size 64. Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) is used for optimization, with learning rate 2 × 10 −4 . We pretrain the captioning model for the maximum 20 epochs, then use the reinforcement learning to train it for 20 epochs and test on the best model on the validation set.

text: • Leveraging prior knowledge of the hierarchy, we employ conventional structure encoders to model label dependencies in both top-down and bottom-up directions, a setting that has not yet been explored for hierarchical text classification. • We introduce a novel end-to-end hierarchy-aware global model (HiAGM). In addition, we propose two variants for label-wise text features: a hierarchy-aware multi-label attention model (HiAGM-LA) and a hierarchy-aware text feature propagation model (HiAGM-TP). • Experiments show that both HiAGM variants yield consistent gains across multiple datasets when paired with different structure encoders. Our strongest model surpasses the state of the art by 3.25% in Macro-F1 and 0.66% in Micro-F1 on RCV1-V2.
source_text: • With the prior hierarchy knowledge, we adopt typical structure encoders for modeling label dependencies in both top-down and bottomup manners, which has not been investigated for hierarchical text classification. • We propose a novel end-to-end hierarchyaware global model (HiAGM). We further present two variants for label-wise text features, a hierarchy-aware multi-label attention model (HiAGM-LA) and a hierarchy-aware text feature propagation model (HiAGM-TP). • We empirically demonstrate that both variants of HiAGM achieve consistent improvements on various datasets when using different structure encoders. Our best model outperforms the state-of-the-art model by 3.25% of Macro-F1 and 0.66% of Micro-F1 on RCV1-V2.

text:
Upon the couch the body lay
Wrapped in the depth of slumber:
Its features were fixed and meaningless,
Yet animal life was there,
And every organ yet performed
Its natural functions: ’twas a sight
Of wonder to behold the body and soul.
The self-same lineaments, the same
Marks of identity were there:
Yet, oh, how different! One aspires to Heaven,
Pants for its sempiternal heritage,
And ever-changing, ever-rising still,
Wantons in endless being.
The other, for a time the unwilling sport
Of circumstance and passion, struggles on;
Fleets through its sad duration rapidly:
Then, like an useless and worn-out machine,
Rots, perishes, and passes.

text:
Spirit of Nature! here  
In this unending wilderness  
Of worlds, beneath whose folded vastness  
Even high-wing'd Fancy reels,  
Here stands thy meetest shrine.  
Yet not the lightest leaf  
That trembles at the passing gale  
Is less alive with thee,—  
Yet not the humblest worm  
That hides in graves and feeds upon the dead  
Less partakes thy eternal breath.  
Spirit of Nature! thou  
Imperishable as this glorious scene,  
Here stands thy meetest shrine.
source_text:
Spirit of Nature! here
In this interminable wilderness
Of worlds, at whose involved immensity
Even soaring fancy staggers,
Here is thy fitting temple.
Yet not the lightest leaf
That quivers to the passing breeze
Is less instinct with thee,—
Yet not the meanest worm.
That lurks in graves and fattens on the dead,
Less shares thy eternal breath.
Spirit of Nature! thou
Imperishable as this glorious scene,
Here is thy fitting temple.

text: We compare the performance of MAG-BERT and MAG-XLNet to a variety of state-of-the-art models for multimodal language analysis. These models are trained using extracted BERT and XLNet word embeddings as their language input: TFN (Tensor Fusion Network) explicitly models both intra-modality and inter-modality dynamics by creating a multidimensional tensor that captures unimodal, bimodal and trimodal interactions across three modalities. MARN (Multi-attention Recurrent Network) models view-specific interactions using hybrid LSTM memories and cross-modal interactions using a Multi-Attention Block (MAB) (Zadeh et al., 2018c) . MFN (Memory Fusion Network) has three separate LSTMs to model each modality separately and a multi-view gated memory to synchronize among them (Zadeh et al., 2018a) .

text: We employ AllenNLP’s wrapper around HuggingFace’s Transformers code 1 for our implementation 2. For general-domain datasets, we initialize models with BERT-base-cased weights, and for clinical datasets we use bio-BERT weights (Lee et al., 2019) as initialization. Our analysis uses cased models, since bio-BERT (Lee et al., 2019) is itself cased. A single learning rate of 2e-5 was used across all experiments. We relied on validation splits supplied by the datasets. When no validation set was provided, as in MADE 1.0, EMRQA, or SQuAD1.1, we reserved 10% of the training data for validation. We set early stopping patience to 5, with each epoch comprising 20,000 steps. Rather than negative log likelihood (NLL), we monitored the final evaluation metric for training and early stopping. This was done to lessen the mis-calibration of the underlying p θ model, given Guo et al. (2017)’s observation that neural networks overfit on NLL. The implementation details for each experiment are given in the following subsections.
source_text: We use AllenNLP's wrapper with HuggingFace's Transformers code 1 for our implementation 2 . We use BERT-base-cased weights as the initialization for general-domain datasets and bio-BERT weights (Lee et al., 2019) as the initialization for clinical datasets. We use cased models for our analysis, since bio-BERT (Lee et al., 2019) uses cased models. A common learning rate of 2e-5 was used for all experiments. We used validation data splits provided by the datasets. In cases where the validation dataset was not provided, such as MADE 1.0, EMRQA or SQuAD1.1, we use 10% of the training data as the validation data. We use a patience of 5 for early stopping the model, with each epoch consisting of 20,000 steps. We use the final evaluation metric instead of negative log likelihood (NLL) to monitor and early stop the training. This is to reduce the mis-calibration of the underlying p θ model, since Guo et al. (2017) observe that neural nets overfit on NLL. The implementation for each experiment is provided in the following subsections.

Nature never did betray
The heart that loved her; 'tis her privilege,
Through all the years of this our life, to lead

Model 2: Phone End-to-End. Our final model employs phone-factored embeddings, in which trainable embeddings of phone features are concatenated with the standard speech feature-vector input. Since phone durations vary and typically extend over more than one filterbank feature, or frame, adjacent filterbank features may share the same predicted phone label; in the example in Figure 1, /R/ covers three frames, or filterbank features. We note that this approach preserves the original speech-feature sequence length. It links similar feature vectors at the corpus level, because all filterbank features with the same phone alignment (e.g. /OH/) receive the same concatenated trainable phone embedding. In MT and NER, concatenating trainable embeddings of linguistic features to words, such as morphemes and phones, has been shown to improve models’ ability to generalize (Sennrich and Haddow, 2016; Chaudhary et al., 2018). Whereas those studies appended finer-grained information to connect words with similar lower-level structure, we use phone embeddings to connect higher-level structure to similar but distinct speech feature vectors globally across a corpus.

Following (He and Glass, 2019) , a list of malicious targets are created to test whether negative training can teach the model not to generate sentences in the list. However, in addition to prevent the model from generating targets in a specific list, it is also important to check whether negative training generalizes to other malicious targets. So, a test target list which contains similar but different targets from the training list are also created to test generalization. The training and test lists each contain 0.5k targets. It is also interesting to investigate whether using more malicious targets for negative training can lower the hit rate on the test list. Towards that end, we train a seq2seq paraphrase model using the paraNMT data-set (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017) , with a model of the same structure as described in Section 2. Then, the paraphrase model is used to generate paraphrases of the malicious targets in the training target list 8 for augmentation. In our experiments, the training list without augmentation is first used for negative training, then it is augmented with 0.5k or 2k paraphrased targets respectively (1 or 4 paraphrase copies for each training target sentence). Samples of the malicious targets are shown in Table 2 . The same training, augmented training and test list are used for all three data-sets, and there is no sequence-level overlap between training lists (augmented or not) and the test list.

text: We conduct experiments on image captioning (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , investigating benefits brought by the Guider network. In image captioning, instead of using a discriminator to define final rewards for generated sentence, we adopt evaluation metrics computed based on human references. The final rewards appear more important as they contain reference (ground-truth) information. Feature-matching rewards work as a regularizer of the final rewards. We call our model in this setting a guider-matching sequence training (GMST) model. An overview of GMST is provided in the Appendix. We test our proposed model on the MS COCO dataset (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , containing 123,287 images in total. Each image is annotated with at least 5 captions. Following Karpathy's split (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , 5,000 images are used for both validation and testing. We report BLEU-k (k from 1 to 4), CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015) , and ME-TEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) scores. We consider two settings: (i) using a pre-trained 152layer ResNet (He et al., 2016) for feature extraction, where we take the output of the 2048-way pool5 layer from ResNet-152, pretrained on the ImageNet dataset; and (ii) using semantic tags detected from the image as features . We use an LSTM with 512 hidden units with mini-batches of size 64. Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) is used for optimization, with learning rate 2 × 10 −4 . We pretrain the captioning model for the maximum 20 epochs, then use the reinforcement learning to train it for 20 epochs and test on the best model on the validation set.

text: Question generation (QG), or Question-Answer pair generation (QAG), is a popular approach to overcome this data scarcity challenge. Some of the recent works resort to semi-supervised learning, by leveraging large amount of unlabeled text (e.g. Wikipedia) to generate synthetic QA pairs with the help of QG systems (Tang et al., 2017; Tang et al., 2018; Sachan and Xing, 2018) . However, existing QG systems have overlooked an important point that generating QA pairs from a context consisting of unstructured texts, is essentially a one-to-many problem. Sequence-tosequence models are known to generate generic sequences (Zhao et al., 2017a) without much variety, as they are trained with maximum likelihood estimation. This is highly suboptimal for QAG since the contexts given to the model often contain richer information that could be exploited to generate multiple QA pairs.

Averaged over all test examples, Backoff can solve 55.1% of the problems within a budget of 100, which is approximately 10% higher than the previous work. On unseen workers (problems), the top 11 (top 52) candidates of Backoff solve the same fraction of problems as the top 3000 candidates of the best-performing algorithm in Kulal et al. (2019).

The results can be seen in Figure 5 and Table 1 , where we use the constraint type as a shorthand for the search algorithm under this constraint. Without constraints, the baseline algorithm performs especially poorly because it needs syntactic context to select relevant code pieces for 26% of the lines with empty pseudocode. "Previous" refers to the previous state of the art model. Figure 5(d) , the lead of SymTable on Syntactic grows linearly: the more these two algorithms search, the more budget is needed by Syntactic to reach the same level as SymTable. Syntactic needs nearly 600 more budget to have comparable performance with SymTable that uses 400 budget. We notice that all of our constrained search methods outperform the previous state-of-the-art. Averaged across all test examples, Backoff can solve 55.1% of the problems within 100 budget, which is ≈ 10% higher than the previous work. text: To estimate how many HIGH RCs were required in training for a HIGH bias to emerge, we first forced all RC attachment in the training set to be HIGH attachment. We then varied, in increments of 10 RC sentences at a time, the proportion of RC sentences relative to filler sentences during training. For each training condition, that is, each RC proportion, we trained 5 RNNs. This experiment yielded a lower bound on the number of HIGH RCs necessary in the training data to overcome any recency bias in the RNNs when every RC displayed HIGH attachment. When only 0.017% of the data, corresponding to 20 sentences, contained RCs with HIGH attachment, the test-set surprisal difference between HIGH and LOW attachment differed significantly from zero (p < 10−5, BayesFactor (BF) > 100),10 with a negative mean difference (µ = −2.24). These findings suggest that the models could learn a HIGH bias from just 20/120000 examples of HIGH RC attachment.
source_text: To determine how many HIGH RCs were needed in training to learn a HIGH bias, we first constrained all the RC attachment in the training data to HIGH attachment. Then, we varied the proportion (in increments of 10 RC sentences at a time) of RC sentences to filler sentences during training. We trained 5 RNNs for each training configuration (i.e. each proportion of RCs). This experiment provided a lower bound on the number of HIGH RCs needed in the training data to overcome any RNN recency bias when all RCs exhibited HIGH attachment. When as little as 0.017% (20 sentences) of the data contained RCs with HIGH attachment, the test difference in surprisal between HIGH and LOW attachment significantly differed from zero (p < 10 −5 , BayesFactor (BF) > 100), 10 with a mean difference less than zero (µ = −2.24). These results indicate that the models were able to acquire a HIGH bias with only 20/120000 examples of HIGH RC attachment.

text: To gain a clearer understanding of the various kinds of feedback supplied by our annotators, we randomly sample 200 examples from the dataset and label them according to feedback type. We begin by assigning each instance to one of three categories: (1) Complete: the feedback fully specifies how the predicted SQL should be corrected, (2) Partial: the feedback suggests a way to correct the predicted SQL, but only incompletely, and (3) Paraphrase: the feedback is a restatement of the original question. In the sample, 81.5% of the feedback was Complete, 13.5% was Partial, and 5.0% was Paraphrase. Examples of each feedback type are provided in Table 2. On closer examination of the Partial and Paraphrase cases, we find that they mostly arise when the distance between the predicted and gold SQL is large, i.e., in cases of major parsing errors. In such situations, annotators either choose to provide partial feedback that at least fixes some of the errors, or opt to rewrite the question in an alternative form.
source_text: To better understand the different types of feedback our annotators provided, we sample 200 examples from the dataset, and annotate them with the type of the feedback. We start by assigning the feedback to one of three categories: (1) Complete: the feedback fully describes how the predicted SQL can be corrected , (2) Partial: the feedback describes a way to correct the predicted SQL but only partially and (3) Paraphrase: the feedback is a paraphrase of the original question. The sample had 81.5% for Complete, 13.5% for Partial and 5.0% for Paraphrase feedback. Examples of each type of feedback are shown in Table 2 . Upon further inspection of the partial and paraphrase feedback, we observe that they mostly happen when the distance between the predicted and gold SQL is high (major parsing errors). As such, annotators opt for providing partial feedback (that would at least correct some of the mistakes) or decide to rewrite the question in a different way.

text: Natural Language Understanding (NLU) denotes the broad task of mapping natural language into formal representations. One research direction in the dialogue community seeks to detect slot-value pairs expressed in user utterances by casting the problem as a classification task (Henderson et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2014; Mrkšić et al., 2017; Vodolán et al., 2017). Another line of work concentrates on transforming single-turn user utterances into more structured meaning representations as a semantic parsing task (Zettlemoyer and Collins, 2005; Jia and Liang, 2016; Dong and Lapata, 2018; Damonte et al., 2019).

Dark amid thrones of light.

How concerted with itself,
How far from any alarms, --

With hues of majesty,

source_text:
Of bronze and blaze
The north, to-night!
So adequate its forms,
So preconcerted with itself,
So distant to alarms, --
An unconcern so sovereign
To universe, or me,
It paints my simple spirit
With tints of majesty,
Till I take vaster attitudes,
And strut upon my stem,
Disdaining men and oxygen,
For arrogance of them.

text:
Ye wild-eyed Muses, sing the Twins of Jove,  
Whom fair-ankled Leda, blent in love  
With mighty Saturn’s heaven-obscuring Child,  
On Taygetus, that lofty mountain wild,  
Bore in gladness: gentle Pollux, free from blame,  
And steed-subduing Castor, heirs of fame.  
These are the Powers who earth-born mortals save  
And ships, whose swiftness skims along the wave.  
When wintry tempests o’er the savage sea  
Are roaring, and the sailors fearfully  
Invoke the Twins of Jove with prayer and vow,  
Assembled in affright upon the lofty prow,  
And sacrifice white lambs,—the wind, and then  
The huge wave bursting close behind again,  
Even then beneath the weltering waters bear  
The staggering ship—they suddenly appear,  
On yellow wings sweeping athwart the sky,  
And lull the blasts to voiceless tranquility,  
And strew the waves upon the white sea’s bed,  
Fair omen of the voyage; from toil and dread  
The sailors rest, rejoicing at the sight,  
And plough the quiet sea in safe delight.
source_text:
Ye wild-eyed Muses, sing the Twins of Jove,
Whom the fair-ankled Leda, mixed in love
With mighty Saturn’s Heaven-obscuring Child,
On Taygetus, that lofty mountain wild,
Brought forth in joy: mild Pollux, void of blame,
And steed-subduing Castor, heirs of fame.
These are the Powers who earth-born mortals save
And ships, whose flight is swift along the wave.
When wintry tempests o’er the savage sea
Are raging, and the sailors tremblingly
Call on the Twins of Jove with prayer and vow,
Gathered in fear upon the lofty prow,
And sacrifice with snow-white lambs,—the wind
And the huge billow bursting close behind,
Even then beneath the weltering waters bear
The staggering ship—they suddenly appear,
On yellow wings rushing athwart the sky,
And lull the blasts in mute tranquillity,
And strew the waves on the white Ocean’s bed,
Fair omen of the voyage; from toil and dread
The sailors rest, rejoicing in the sight,
And plough the quiet sea in safe delight.

Thou Friend, whose presence on my wintry heart
Fell, like bright Spring upon some herbless plain;
How beautiful and calm and free thou wert
In thy young wisdom, when the mortal chain
Of Custom thou didst burst and rend in twain,
And walked as free as light the clouds among,
Which many an envious slave then breathed in vain
From his dim dungeon, and my spirit sprung
To meet thee from the woes which had begirt it long!

What wreath for Lamia? What for Lycius?
What for the sage, old Apollonius?
Upon her aching forehead be there hung
The leaves of willow and of adder's tongue;
And for the youth, quick, let us strip for him
The thyrsus, that his watching eyes may swim
Into forgetfulness; and, for the sage,
Let spear-grass and the spiteful thistle wage
War on his temples. Do not all charms fly
At the mere touch of cold philosophy?
There was an awful rainbow once in heaven:
We know her woof, her texture; she is given
In the dull catalogue of common things.
Philosophy will clip an Angel's wings,
Conquer all mysteries by rule and line,
Empty the haunted air, and gnomed mine--
Unweave a rainbow, as it erewhile made
The tender-person'd Lamia melt into a shade.

Given a large collection of task-specific unlabeled data, we can transfer the teacher's knowledge to the student via its hidden representations. However, this poses a challenge in our setting as the teacher and student models have different architectures with disparate output spaces. Consider x u ∈ D u to be the set of unlabeled instances. We will later discuss the choice of the teacher layer l and its impact on distillation. text: Reference-free MT evaluation. Recently, interest has been increasing in reference-free MT evaluation (Ma et al., 2019), also known as “quality estimation” (QE) within the MT community. In this setting, evaluation metrics perform a semantic comparison between system translations and the source sentences directly. The appeal of automatic reference-free MT evaluation is evident: it does not require human annotation or parallel data. To address this task, Popović et al. (2011) use a bag-of-word translation model to estimate translation quality by summing the likelihoods of aligned word pairs between source and translation texts. Specia et al. (2013) estimate translation quality with language-agnostic linguistic features extracted from source-language texts and system translations. Lo et al. (2014) propose XMEANT as a crosslingual, reference-free variant of MEANT, a metric grounded in semantic frames. Lo (2019) later extended this approach by incorporating M-BERT embeddings. The resulting metric, YiSi-2, assesses system translations by summing similarity scores over word pairs that are best-aligned mutual translations. YiSi-2-SRL optionally adds an extra similarity score based on alignments over semantic structures (e.g., semantic roles and frames). Although both metrics are reference-free, YiSi-2-SRL is not resource-lean because it requires a semantic parser for both languages. Furthermore, unlike our proposed metrics, they neither address misalignment in cross-lingual embedding spaces nor incorporate a target-side language model, which we consider crucial components.
source_text: Reference-free MT evaluation. Recently, there has been a growing interest in reference-free MT evaluation (Ma et al., 2019) , also referred to as "quality estimation" (QE) in the MT community. In this setup, evaluation metrics semantically compare system translations directly to the source sentences. The attractiveness of automatic referencefree MT evaluation is obvious: it does not require any human effort or parallel data. To approach this task, Popović et al. (2011) exploit a bag-ofword translation model to estimate translation quality, which sums over the likelihoods of aligned word-pairs between source and translation texts. Specia et al. (2013) estimate translation quality using language-agnostic linguistic features extracted from source lanuage texts and system translations. Lo et al. (2014) introduce XMEANT as a crosslingual reference-free variant of MEANT, a metric based on semantic frames. Lo (2019) extended this idea by leveraging M-BERT embeddings. The resulting metric, YiSi-2, evaluates system translations by summing similarity scores over words pairs that are best-aligned mutual translations. YiSi-2-SRL optionally combines an additional similarity score based on the alignment over the semantic structures (e.g., semantic roles and frames). Both metrics are reference-free, but YiSi-2-SRL is not resource-lean as it requires a semantic parser for both languages. Moreover, in contrast to our proposed metrics, they do not mitigate the misalignment of cross-lingual embedding spaces and do not integrate a target-side language model, which we identify to be crucial components.

text: Some methods collect human ratings on a small number of summaries to train an evaluation function. Peyrard et al. (2017) ; Peyrard and Gurevych (2018) propose to learn an evaluation function from Pyramid and Responsiveness scores, by using classic supervised learning methods with hand-crafted features. ShafieiBavani et al. (2018b) use the same idea but design corpus based and lexical re-source based word embeddings to build the features. Böhm et al. (2019) train a BERT-based evaluation function with 2,500 human ratings for 500 machinegenerated summaries from the CNN/DailyMail dataset; their method correlates better with human ratings than ROUGE and BLEU. However, as their method is designed for evaluating single-document summaries, it correlates poorly with the Pyramid scores for multi-document summaries (see §3).

SpanBERT show about the same error rate on the groups per:loc and same nertag&positive. They differ, however, in which examples they predict correctly: For per:loc, 78.6% are predicted by both models, and 21.4% are predicted by only one of the models. For same nertag&positive, 12.8% of the examples are predicted by only of the models. The two models thus seem to identify complementary information. One difference between the models is that KnowBERT has access to entity information, while SpanBERT masks entity spans. To test how much the two models balance context and argument information, we apply rewriting to alter the instances belonging to a group and observe the impact on performance. We use two strategies: (1) we remove all tokens outside the span between head and tail argument (outside), and (2) we remove all tokens between the two arguments (between). We find that SpanBERT's performance on per:loc drops from 62.1 F1 to 57.7 (outside) and 43.3 (between), whereas Know-BERT's score decreases from 63.7 F1 to 60.9 and 50.1, respectively. On same nertag&positive, we observe a drop from 89.2 F1 to 58.2 (outside) and 47.7 (between) for SpanBERT. Know-BERT achieves a score of 89.4, which drops to 83.8 and 49.0. The larger drop in performance on same nertag&positive suggests that SpanBERT, which uses entity masking, focuses more on the context, whereas KnowBERT focuses on the entity content because the model has access to the arguments. Surprisingly, both models show similar performance on the full test set (Table 5 ). This suggests that combining both approaches may further improve RE performance.

text: Following He and Glass (2019), we construct a list of malicious targets to test whether negative training can teach the model not to generate sentences from that list. However, beyond preventing the model from producing targets in a particular list, it is also important to examine whether negative training generalizes to other malicious targets. Accordingly, we also create a test target list containing targets that are similar to, but different from, those in the training list in order to evaluate generalization. The training and test lists each contain 0.5k targets. It is furthermore of interest to investigate whether using a larger number of malicious targets for negative training can reduce the hit rate on the test list. To this end, we train a seq2seq paraphrase model on the paraNMT data-set (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017), using a model with the same architecture as described in Section 2. The paraphrase model is then used to generate paraphrases of the malicious targets in training target list 8 for augmentation. In our experiments, the unaugmented training list is first used for negative training, and then it is augmented with 0.5k or 2k paraphrased targets, respectively (i.e., 1 or 4 paraphrase copies for each training target sentence). Examples of the malicious targets are shown in Table 2. The same training, augmented training, and test lists are used for all three data-sets, and there is no sequence-level overlap between the training lists, whether augmented or not, and the test list.

However, in addition to prevent the model from generating targets in a specific list, it is also important to check whether negative training generalizes to other malicious targets. So, a test target list which contains similar but different targets from the training list are also created to test generalization. The training and test lists each contain 0.5k targets. It is also interesting to investigate whether using more malicious targets for negative training can lower the hit rate on the test list. Towards that end, we train a seq2seq paraphrase model using the paraNMT data-set (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017) , with a model of the same structure as described in Section 2. Then, the paraphrase model is used to generate paraphrases of the malicious targets in the training target list 8 for augmentation. In our experiments, the training list without augmentation is first used for negative training, then it is augmented with 0.5k or 2k paraphrased targets respectively (1 or 4 paraphrase copies for each training target sentence). Samples of the malicious targets are shown in Table 2 . The same training, augmented training and test list are used for all three data-sets, and there is no sequence-level overlap between training lists (augmented or not) and the test list.

As one by one the stately players vanish,
From that vast drama on history’s endless stage,
That glaring, partisan scene of war and peace—of old and new in conflict,
Wrought through rage, through fear, through midnight dread, and many a weary pause;
All gone—and since, in numberless graves receding, softening,

source_text:
As one by one withdraw the lofty actors,
From that great play on history’s stage eterne,
That lurid, partial act of war and peace--of old and new contending,
Fought out through wrath, fears, dark dismays, and many a long suspense;
All past--and since, in countless graves receding, mellowing,
Victor’s and vanquish’d--Lincoln’s and Lee’s--now thou with them,
Man of the mighty days--and equal to the days!
Thou from the prairies!--tangled and many-vein’d and hard has been thy part,
To admiration has it been enacted!

text: Motivation: Pre-trained language models have shown state-of-the-art performance for various natural language processing applications like text classification, named entity recognition and questionanswering. A significant challenge facing practitioners is how to deploy these huge models in practice. For instance, models like BERT Large (Devlin et al., 2019) , GPT 2 (Radford et al., 2019) , Megatron (Shoeybi et al., 2019) and T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) have 340M , 1.5B, 8.3B and 11B parameters respectively. Although these models are trained offline, during prediction we need to traverse the deep neural network architecture stack involving a large number of parameters. This significantly increases latency and memory requirements.

text: For RL-based text generation, the majority of existing studies adopt a model-free framework, which has been criticized for its high variance and low sample efficiency (Sutton and Barto, 1998). By contrast, although model-based RL methods do not exhibit these drawbacks, they are generally difficult to train in complex environments. Moreover, the capacity of the environment model typically constrains the learned policy. Recent advances in model-based RL (Gu et al., 2016; Kurutach et al., 2018; Nagabandi et al., 2017) integrate the strengths of both paradigms and have yielded better performance by learning a model-free policy with the aid of an environment model. In addition, model-based RL has recently been applied to tasks with extremely sparse rewards through curiosity-driven methods (Pathak et al., 2017).
source_text: For RL-based text generation, most existing works rely on a model-free framework, which has been criticized for its high variance and poor sample efficiency (Sutton and Barto, 1998) . On the other hand, while model-based RL methods do not suffer from these issues, they are usually difficult to train in complex environments. Further, a learned policy is usually restricted by the capacity of an environment model. Recent developments on model-based RL (Gu et al., 2016; Kurutach et al., 2018; Nagabandi et al., 2017) combine the advantages of these two approaches, and have achieved improved performance by learning a model-free policy, assisted by an environment model. In addition, model-based RL has been employed recently to solve problems with extremely sparse rewards, with curiosity-driven methods (Pathak et al., 2017) .
