text: Identifying BABY-STEPs Our learning framework requires an agent to execute microinstructions (i.e., BABY-STEPs). However, existing datasets (Anderson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019) do not offer fine-grained segmentations of lengthy instructions. We therefore adopt a template-matching strategy to combine adjacent sentences into BABY-STEPs. First, we extract the noun phrase via POS tagging. We then apply heuristic rules to divide a long instruction into shorter segments based on punctuation and landmark phrases (i.e., words denoting concrete objects). We provide the full details in the Appendix.
source_text: Identifying BABY-STEPs Our learning approach requires an agent to follow microinstructions (i.e., the BABY-STEPs). Therefore, we use a template matching approach to aggregate consecutive sentences into BABY-STEPs. First, we extract the noun phrase using POS tagging. Then, we employs heuristic rules to chunk a long instruction into shorter segments according to punctuation and landmark phrase (i.e., words for concrete objects). We document the details in the Appendix.

text: We observe that semi-structured premises forced annotators to call upon world knowledge and common sense (KCS); 48.75% instances in the Dev set require KCS. (In comparison, in the MultiNLI data, KCS is needed in 25.72% of examples.) We conjecture that this is because information about the entities and their types is not explicitly stated in tables, and have to be inferred. To do so, our annotators relied on their knowledge about the world including information about weather, seasons, and widely known social and cultural norms and facts. An example of such common sense is the hypothesis that "X was born in summer" for a person whose date of birth is in May in New York. We expect that the INFOTABS data can serve as a basis for studying common sense reasoning alongside other recent work such as that of Talmor et al. text: We now describe how knowledge of narrative structure is incorporated into our summarization models. For both models, this knowledge is conveyed through a network pre-trained on the TRIPOD 4 dataset introduced by Papalampidi et al. (2019). This dataset comprises 99 movies annotated with turning points. TPs are pivotal narrative events that shape plot progression and are situated between successive acts, or thematic units. It is commonly assumed (Cutting, 2016) that a film consists of six acts (Figure 1), each separated by a turning point (shown as arrows in the figure). Moreover, each of the five TPs has a specific narrative role: we list each TP together with its definition as given in screenwriting theory (Hauge, 2017) and adopted by Papalampidi et al. (2019) in Table 1 (see Appendix A for a more detailed account of narrative structure theory). Papalampidi et al. (2019) detect movie scenes that align with these key events as a way to analyze the narrative structure of films. They first gather sentence-level TP annotations for plot synopses and then project them onto screenplays using distant supervision, producing silver-standard labels. We use this silver-standard dataset to pretrain a network for TP identification.
source_text: We now explain how to inject knowledge about narrative structure into our summarization models. For both models, such knowledge is transferred via a network pre-trained on the TRIPOD 4 dataset introduced by Papalampidi et al. (2019) . This dataset contains 99 movies annotated with turning points. TPs are key events in a narrative that define the progression of the plot and occur between consecutive acts (thematic units). It is often assumed (Cutting, 2016) that there are six acts in a film (Figure 1 ), each delineated by a turning point (arrows in the figure). Each of the five TPs has also a well-defined function in the narrative: we present each TP alongside with its definition as stated in screenwriting theory (Hauge, 2017 ) and adopted by Papalampidi et al. (2019) in Table 1 (see Appendix A for a more detailed description of narrative structure theory). Papalampidi et al. (2019) identify scenes in movies that correspond to these key events as a means for analyzing the narrative 2019structure of movies. They collect sentence-level TP annotations for plot synopses and subsequently project them via distant supervision onto screenplays, thereby creating silver-standard labels. We utilize this silver-standard dataset in order to pretrain a network which performs TP identification.

Optimization tools, such as SVM-struct (Joachims, 2008), are concerned with parameter estimation. Model libraries, such as CRFSuite (Okazaki, 2007), CRF++ (Kudo, 2005), and NCRF++ (Yang and Zhang, 2018), provide inference for a fixed collection of widely used models, typically linear-chain CRFs. General-purpose inference libraries, such as PyStruct (Müller and Behnke, 2014) or TurboParser (Martins et al., 2010), rely on external solvers for inference, chiefly MAP inference, including integer linear programming solvers and ADMM. Probabilistic programming languages, for instance languages integrated with deep learning such as Pyro (Bingham et al., 2019), enable specification and inference over some discrete domains. More ambitiously, inference libraries such as Dyna (Eisner et al., 2004) support declarative descriptions of dynamic programming algorithms to enable inference for generic algorithms. Torch-Struct adopts a different strategy, integrating a library of optimized structured distributions into a vectorized deep learning framework. We start by motivating this design with a case study.
source_text: Optimization tools, such as SVM-struct (Joachims, 2008) , focus on parameter estimation. Model libraries, such as CRFSuite (Okazaki, 2007) , CRF++ (Kudo, 2005) , or NCRF++ (Yang and Zhang, 2018) , implement inference for a fixed set of popular models, usually linear-chain CRFs. General-purpose inference libraries, such as PyStruct (Müller and Behnke, 2014) or Tur-boParser (Martins et al., 2010), utilize external solvers for (primarily MAP) inference such as integer linear programming solvers and ADMM. Probabilistic programming languages, for example languages that integrate with deep learning such as Pyro (Bingham et al., 2019) , allow for specification and inference over some discrete domains. Most ambitiously, inference libraries such as Dyna (Eisner et al., 2004) allow for declarative specifications of dynamic programming algorithms to support inference for generic algorithms. Torch-Struct takes a different approach and integrates a library of optimized structured distributions into a vectorized deep learning system. We begin by motivating this approach with a case study.

MEPHISTOPHELES:
In truth, I generally go about
In strict incognito; and yet one likes
To wear one’s orders upon gala days.
I have no ribbon at my knee; but here
At home, the cloven foot is honourable.
See you that snail there?—she comes creeping up,
And with her feeling eyes hath smelt out something.
I could not, if I would, mask myself here.
Come now, we’ll go about from fire to fire:
I’ll be the Pimp, and you shall be the Lover.
[TO SOME OLD WOMEN, WHO ARE SITTING ROUND A HEAP OF GLIMMERING COALS.]
Old gentlewomen, what do you do out here?
You ought to be with the young rioters
Right in the thickest of the revelry—
But every one is best content at home.

text: We now explain how to inject knowledge about narrative structure into our summarization models. For both models, such knowledge is transferred via a network pre-trained on the TRIPOD 4 dataset introduced by Papalampidi et al. (2019) . This dataset contains 99 movies annotated with turning points. TPs are key events in a narrative that define the progression of the plot and occur between consecutive acts (thematic units). It is often assumed (Cutting, 2016) that there are six acts in a film (Figure 1 ), each delineated by a turning point (arrows in the figure). Each of the five TPs has also a well-defined function in the narrative: we present each TP alongside with its definition as stated in screenwriting theory (Hauge, 2017 ) and adopted by Papalampidi et al. (2019) in Table 1 (see Appendix A for a more detailed description of narrative structure theory). Papalampidi et al. (2019) identify scenes in movies that correspond to these key events as a means for analyzing the narrative 2019structure of movies. They collect sentence-level TP annotations for plot synopses and subsequently project them via distant supervision onto screenplays, thereby creating silver-standard labels. We utilize this silver-standard dataset in order to pretrain a network which performs TP identification.
